identity.title: Leather and Hide Tanning and Finishing

lens.included_activities: Independent US contract tanneries and leather finishers that repeatedly process customer-owned hides, skins, wet-blue stock, or leather to customer specifications for business customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Tanneries selling only their own finished leather, converters that merely own and arrange processing, captive processing units, fur dressing that is not repeat outsourced work, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat B2B processing revenue, generally quoted by hide, skin, lot, area, or specified finishing job, with the customer retaining ownership of the input material.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS industry combines product manufacturers, converters, and contract processors. The lens is narrowed to contract tanning and finishing because only that model supplies the recurring outsourced service specified by the fixed screen.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for all NAICS 3161 establishments, not wage-weighted AI task exposure for contract processors. The 2024 list is dominated by hands-on shoe/leather, dyeing, material-moving, maintenance, cutting, and coating roles; I assign limited exposure to their documentation, scheduling, inspection-support, and setup tasks and more exposure to supervisors, managers, and office clerks, then widen the range because the published top-occupation table omits the long tail. This likely overstates substitution if small contract shops have more craft work than the industry average.
a.rationale: The industry's largest jobs are physical production roles: BLS reports 690 shoe and leather workers, 210 production supervisors, 180 other textile/apparel/furnishings workers, 150 dyeing-machine operators, and 150 material movers in May 2024. AI can reduce clerical, quoting, production-planning, specification extraction, quality-documentation, and some visual-inspection labor, but it cannot directly perform wet processing, machine tending, material handling, maintenance, or most finishing work.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 8-12 US contract tanneries, including time studies for quoting, color/specification matching, quality inspection, production planning, compliance documentation, and customer service, mapped to demonstrable AI tools.
caveats: BLS publishes this occupation chart for the full industry, not the narrowed contract-processing lens.
The top-ten occupation chart does not provide a complete wage-weighted task distribution.
Visual defect detection is technically plausible but plant-specific image data, lighting, and handling integration can dominate feasibility.

rho.rationale: Over five years, readily digitized work such as order intake, job-ticket creation, scheduling, specification checking, compliance drafting, and customer communication should be implementable, while production control and automated defect grading face integration, data, quality, safety, and change-management constraints. EPA's process description shows numerous chemically and mechanically distinct stages, limiting a software-only rollout.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor-neutral pilots at operating US tanneries measuring adopted labor hours before and after AI-assisted scheduling, machine-vision inspection, lab/color matching, and environmental reporting over at least two seasonal production cycles.
caveats: No current industry adoption survey was found.
Small plants may lack MES/ERP data and staff needed to maintain integrations.
Environmental and customer-quality accountability slows implementation even when a model works in a trial.

e.rationale: Only firms that process customer-owned material qualify. EPA distinguishes regular tanneries, converters, and contract tanneries and says contract finishers are the largest segment within contract operations, but no current source reports the share of LMM firms deriving recurring revenue from contract processing. A minority, centered on specialist finishing and partial-process work, is therefore assumed eligible.
e.change_evidence: A current establishment-level census of the 26 frozen-band firms, using websites, customer terms, UCC filings, and management calls to classify customer-owned-material contract revenue versus own-product sales.
caveats: The NAICS definition includes converters that outsource their own material; those firms do not necessarily supply an external service.
EPA's structural evidence is old and may not describe the much smaller current industry.
Mixed-model firms may have both proprietary leather sales and contract-processing revenue.

s5.bridge: Gallup's 2024 US survey covers employer-business owners across industries, not LMM contract tanneries. It reports 22% of employer owners plan to sell or transfer within five years and 30% among employer owners age 55 or older. I use 22% as the base, with a range spanning lower realization and the older-owner rate. The proxy may overstate qualifying control transfers because intentions may not close and gifts or internal transfers may not produce an acquirable control transaction; it may understate unplanned or strategically initiated sales.
s5.rationale: The best directly timed evidence is Gallup's five-year owner-intention survey. The industry's legacy, specialized plants plausibly skew older, but without industry-specific owner-age data the all-employer observed rate is retained rather than uplifted.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession-plan data for US contract tanneries plus five years of closed transactions from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, BizBuySell, trade advisers, and local M&A attorneys, identifying true control transfers and excluding closures.
caveats: Survey intentions are not completed transactions.
The survey spans all employer industries and business sizes.
Very small eligible-firm counts make realized transfer rates volatile.

q.rationale: Contract processors sell a specified physical result rather than labor hours, so productivity gains can initially remain in plant margin. Repeated B2B quoting, customer concentration, transparent per-hide or per-lot economics, and competition from domestic and foreign processors should pass a meaningful portion back through renewals and bids over five years.
q.change_evidence: An anonymized sample of customer contracts, quote histories, renewal cadence, price-adjustment clauses, gross-margin bridges, customer concentration, and win/loss data from at least five eligible firms.
caveats: No public contract-pricing dataset was found.
Retention varies sharply between commodity wet processing and scarce specialty finishing.
This estimate excludes demand loss and implementation friction, which belong in other primitives.

d5.bridge: BLS/FRED reports nominal sectoral output for all NAICS 3161, not constant-price demand for eligible contract processors. Nominal output was $1,238.741 million in 2021, $1,189.322 million in 2022, $1,217.642 million in 2023, and $1,196.484 million in 2024, a broadly flat nominal path after the 2021 rebound. I combine that weak recent path with EPA's documented long-run pressures from synthetics, imports, and regulation and center year-five real demand below current. Nominal data likely overstate quantity growth when prices rise, while specialty reshoring or traceability demand could make the contract niche outperform the whole industry.
d5.rationale: The current service basket serves durable leather end uses, but the domestic base is exposed to imported leather and synthetic substitutes. Recent nominal output is roughly flat rather than clearly expanding, so a modest real contraction is the central case, with a wide band for niche specialty demand.
d5.change_evidence: BLS real sectoral-output history for NAICS 316110, eligible-firm order books and processed-hide volumes by end market, customer sourcing plans, and five-year US leather import and downstream footwear/upholstery production forecasts.
caveats: Sectoral output is nominal and covers product sellers as well as contract processors.
EPA's explanation of long-run decline is dated.
End markets differ: luxury, military, automotive, footwear, and industrial leather can move in different directions.

o.rationale: Nearly all surviving demand still requires a responsible processor to receive and handle variable biological material, run chemical and mechanical steps, assure finish quality, manage wastewater and emissions, and deliver compliant leather. Software may automate decisions and records but does not eliminate the accountable plant operator; only limited customer self-processing or fully autonomous production reduces this share.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews on insourcing plans and evidence from commercially operating autonomous tanning/finishing lines showing end-to-end production without an independent accountable processor.
caveats: The operator may consolidate across fewer, larger plants even if the service quantity remains.
This is a judgment about the need for an accountable operator, not the number of workers required.
Customers that vertically integrate finishing would reduce the eligible outsourced operator share.

narrative.executive_view: The relevant opportunity is a narrow subset of a small, specialized manufacturing industry: contract tanneries and finishers processing customer-owned material. Physical production and environmental accountability make the operator durable, but they also cap direct AI labor substitution and raise integration risk.
narrative.ai_impact: AI is most useful around the plant: translating customer specifications into job tickets, quoting, scheduling, matching recipes and colors, drafting compliance records, assisting visual quality inspection, and handling customer communication. The core wet and dry processing steps remain equipment- and labor-mediated, so avoided hiring is more plausible than broad labor replacement.
narrative.value_capture: Per-hide, per-lot, or job-based pricing lets a processor retain savings until a bid or renewal resets economics. Specialty quality, short runs, and compliance can protect margin, while concentrated buyers, transparent conversion pricing, and offshore alternatives accelerate sharing.
narrative.firm_availability: Eligibility is the central gating issue because NAICS 316110 includes regular product-selling tanneries and converters as well as contract processors. General employer-owner evidence suggests meaningful five-year transfer intent, but the eligible pool implied by the frozen 26-firm estimate may contain only a handful of firms.
narrative.demand_durability: Domestic demand faces synthetics, imports, environmental burdens, and a long history of facility decline. The remaining specialty work may be defensible, and almost all surviving tanning or finishing demand still needs an accountable physical operator, but a modest real-volume decline is the base case.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the current share of LMM firms that truly process customer-owned material, contract-level pricing power, end-market concentration, environmental liabilities, and whether plant data are sufficient for machine vision and workflow automation. A single large customer loss or remediation obligation can overwhelm modest labor savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Durable need for a compliant physical processor supporting repeat specialty B2B workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: The eligible contract-processing population is unmeasured and probably small within a structurally pressured domestic industry.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: Leather and Hide Tanning and Finishing
sources.supports: Defines NAICS 316110 as tanning, currying, and finishing hides and skins, having others process them on contract, and dyeing or dressing furs; supports the industry's mixed business models and lens narrowing.

sources.title: BLS OEWS Data Tables for Industry Employment Charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Reports the ten largest occupations and employment counts in leather and hide tanning and finishing, including 690 shoe/leather workers, 210 production supervisors, 180 other textile/apparel workers, 150 dyeing-machine operators, and 150 material movers.

sources.title: EPA AP-42 Section 9.15: Leather Tanning
sources.supports: Describes the many physical and chemical stages of tanning and finishing, reports that chrome tanning was about 90% of US production, and notes long-run facility decline associated with synthetic substitutes, imports, and environmental regulation.

sources.title: BLS via FRED: Sectoral Output for Leather and Hide Tanning and Finishing
sources.supports: Reports nominal annual sectoral output of $1,238.741 million in 2021, $1,189.322 million in 2022, $1,217.642 million in 2023, and $1,196.484 million in 2024, and defines the series.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports from a fall 2024 US survey that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, rising to 30% among employer owners age 55 or older; also reports 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people 55 or older.

sources.title: EPA Assessment of Industrial Hazardous Waste Practices: Leather Tanning and Finishing Industry
sources.supports: Distinguishes regular tanneries, converters, and contract tanneries; defines contract tanneries as processors of material owned by another party and states that contract leather finishers made up the largest segment of contract operations.
